claims.claim_text: The opening anecdote is framed as a specific January 2023 incident at a Fortune 500 financial services company.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: This is a specific real-world framing with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote claims a specific enterprise used a long-lived IAM user with AdministratorAccess for production EKS access instead of cross-account role chains.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: An engineer needed to debug a production issue in a Kubernetes cluster running in a different AWS account. The standard process: log into the management console, switch roles to the production account, navigate to EKS, download the kubeconfig, and authenticate with the cluster. The "role switch" used a long-lived IAM user with `AdministratorAccess` in the production account because the team had never gotten around to building proper cross-account role assumption chains.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In some organizations, engineers still reach production clusters through ad hoc console workflows and shared high-privilege identities instead of federated cross-account role chains.
claims.rationale: It presents an unattributed, specific operational narrative as a real incident.

claims.claim_text: The anecdote claims specific breach details including shared keys in GitHub, 11 hours of access, 2.3 million records exfiltrated, and $18 million in losses.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: That IAM user's access keys were stored in a shared `.env` file in a private GitHub repo. When an employee left the company and their personal GitHub account was compromised three months later, the attacker found the keys, assumed the production role, and had full admin access to production infrastructure for 11 hours before CloudTrail alerts triggered. The attacker exfiltrated 2.3 million customer records. The breach cost $18 million in regulatory fines, legal fees, and customer notification.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Storing long-lived production credentials in shared repositories creates a high-risk path to compromise: if those credentials are later exposed, an attacker can assume privileged roles, retain access long enough to cause serious damage, and create major legal and customer impact before detection.
claims.rationale: These are fake-precise incident specifics without a source.

claims.claim_text: AWS cross-account access is based on STS role assumption, which returns temporary credentials.
claims.span_hint: section: Cross-Account Role Assumption (AWS)
claims.rationale: The STS AssumeRole API directly documents temporary credentials for role assumption.

claims.claim_text: `aws:PrincipalOrgID` can restrict trust to principals that belong to a specific AWS Organization.
claims.span_hint: section: Cross-Account Role Assumption (AWS) / Stop and think
claims.rationale: AWS documents `aws:PrincipalOrgID` as a global condition key for restricting access to organization members.

claims.claim_text: AWS IAM Identity Center is the recommended centralized way to manage workforce access across multiple AWS accounts, often with external IdPs such as Okta or Microsoft Entra ID.
claims.span_hint: section: IAM Identity Center (AWS SSO)
claims.rationale: AWS guidance explicitly recommends IAM Identity Center for centrally managed multi-account workforce access and names external IdPs.

claims.claim_text: Creating an IAM Identity Center account assignment automatically provisions the permission set into the target account as an IAM role/policy.
claims.span_hint: section: Setting Up IAM Identity Center with Terraform
claims.rationale: The API reference states that successful account assignment provisions the permission set to the account.

claims.claim_text: Workload Identity Federation for GKE is the recommended way for GKE workloads to access Google Cloud APIs without service account key files.
claims.span_hint: section: GCP Workload Identity Federation Across Projects
claims.rationale: Google documents Workload Identity Federation for GKE as the recommended approach and contrasts it with service account keys.

claims.claim_text: A GKE Kubernetes ServiceAccount can impersonate an IAM service account in another Google Cloud project by granting `roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser` and annotating the KSA with `iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account`.
claims.span_hint: section: GCP Workload Identity Federation Across Projects
claims.rationale: The GKE how-to shows cross-project IAM service accounts, the `roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser` binding, and the required annotation.

claims.claim_text: In GKE Workload Identity Federation, namespace and Kubernetes ServiceAccount name are part of the trusted principal identity, so the same service account name in another namespace is a different identity.
claims.span_hint: section: GCP Workload Identity Federation Across Projects / Pause and predict
claims.rationale: Google documents the member format as `serviceAccount:PROJECT_ID.svc.id.goog[NAMESPACE/KSA_NAME]`, which makes namespace part of identity.

claims.claim_text: AKS workload identity uses Kubernetes service account tokens with OIDC federation to obtain Microsoft Entra tokens.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Entra ID and Workload Identity
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS workload identity overview explains the OIDC-based token exchange flow.

claims.claim_text: Microsoft recommends Microsoft Entra Workload ID instead of AKS pod-managed identity, which is deprecated.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Entra ID and Workload Identity
claims.rationale: The AKS pod-managed identity page explicitly says Workload ID replaces pod-managed identity and notes deprecation.

claims.claim_text: Federated identity credentials for Microsoft Entra workload identity use the audience `api://AzureADTokenExchange`.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Entra ID and Workload Identity / Pause and predict
claims.rationale: Microsoft Entra workload identity documentation states this is the recommended audience value.

claims.claim_text: AWS ABAC policies can compare `aws:ResourceTag` and `aws:PrincipalTag` to make runtime authorization decisions based on tags.
claims.span_hint: section: AWS ABAC with Tags
claims.rationale: AWS documents `aws:PrincipalTag` and `aws:ResourceTag` as IAM policy condition keys for tag-based access control.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud IAM Conditions use CEL and can enforce time-based access constraints such as business-hours-only access.
claims.span_hint: section: GCP ABAC with IAM Conditions
claims.rationale: Google's IAM Conditions overview documents CEL and provides time-based condition examples.

claims.claim_text: An organization trail in CloudTrail logs events for all member accounts and begins logging automatically for accounts added later to the organization.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Configurations for Identity Auditing
claims.rationale: AWS documents that organization trails apply to all member accounts and start logging for newly added accounts automatically.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes auditing records security-relevant events and answers questions such as who initiated an action, what happened, and on what resource it happened.
claims.span_hint: section: Centralized Audit Logging for Identity Events / Tracing a Cross-Account Kubernetes Event
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes auditing documentation describes the audit record purpose and fields.

claims.claim_text: S3 Object Lock provides WORM storage, and in compliance mode a protected object version cannot be deleted or overwritten even by the root user until retention expires.
claims.span_hint: section: Centralized Audit Logging for Identity Events / Stop and think
claims.rationale: AWS documents WORM behavior and explicitly states compliance mode blocks deletion or overwrite by any user, including root.

claims.claim_text: AWS IAM Identity Center temporary elevated access (just-in-time access) is time-bound access to perform a specific task with request, approval, and tracking.
claims.span_hint: section: Just-In-Time (JIT) Access
claims.rationale: AWS defines temporary elevated access in these terms.

claims.claim_text: The specific vendor examples for JIT tooling are illustrative rather than load-bearing.
claims.span_hint: section: Implementing JIT with AWS SSO Permission Sets
claims.anchor_text: You can implement a basic JIT system using AWS SSO APIs combined with automation tools like ConductorOne or Indent.
claims.suggested_rewrite: You can implement a basic JIT system using AWS IAM Identity Center APIs combined with a temporary elevated access workflow tool.
claims.rationale: The lesson is valid, but the named vendor examples are not necessary to support the concept.

claims.claim_text: Google Cloud Workload Identity Federation supports external workloads on AWS, Azure, and other OIDC/SAML-capable platforms without requiring service account keys.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / item 2
claims.rationale: Google's workload identity federation overview explicitly lists AWS, Azure, OIDC, and SAML scenarios and frames keyless access as the benefit.

claims.claim_text: The module claims AWS IAM evaluates an average of 2.5 billion authorization requests per second with sub-millisecond latency and local service-endpoint evaluation.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / item 1
claims.anchor_text: 1. **AWS IAM evaluates an average of 2.5 billion authorization requests per second** across all AWS accounts globally. Every API call, every S3 object access, every Lambda invocation goes through IAM evaluation. The system has a design target of sub-millisecond evaluation latency, which is why IAM policies are evaluated locally at the service endpoint rather than at a central authorization server.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **AWS IAM evaluates authorization decisions at very large scale.** Every API call to an AWS service is subject to IAM authorization, so policy evaluation must be designed not to become a central bottleneck.
claims.rationale: The exact throughput and latency figures are not backed by an allowlisted primary source here.

claims.claim_text: The module says confused deputy attacks were first described by Norm Hardy in 1988 and that every cross-account role should use `aws:SourceArn` and `aws:SourceAccount`.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / item 3
claims.anchor_text: 3. **The concept of "confused deputy" attacks** was first described by Norm Hardy in 1988. In cloud IAM, it applies when a service (the "deputy") is tricked into using its own permissions on behalf of an unauthorized caller. AWS mitigates this with the `aws:SourceArn` and `aws:SourceAccount` conditions on trust policies. Every cross-account role should include these conditions to prevent confused deputy attacks.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 3. **A confused deputy attack** occurs when a more-privileged service or account is tricked into acting on behalf of an unauthorized caller. In AWS, the right mitigation depends on the integration: use `sts:ExternalId` for third-party cross-account access, and use `aws:SourceArn` or `aws:SourceAccount` for AWS service principals where the service documentation supports them.
claims.rationale: The historical attribution is off-allowlist here, and the mitigation guidance is overgeneralized and partly incorrect.

claims.claim_text: The module says Microsoft Entra ID processes over 90 billion authentications per day as of 2025 and is the world's largest identity provider by transaction volume.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? / item 4
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Azure Entra ID processes over 90 billion authentications per day** as of 2025, making it the largest identity provider in the world by transaction volume. This includes not just Azure cloud access but Microsoft 365, third-party SaaS applications, and on-premises Active Directory hybrid scenarios. When you federate AKS workload identity through Entra ID, your Kubernetes pods are participating in this same massively distributed identity fabric.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Microsoft Entra ID handles authentication at very large global scale** across Microsoft cloud and SaaS scenarios. When you federate AKS workload identity through Entra ID, your Kubernetes workloads rely on that same identity platform for token exchange.
claims.rationale: The exact count and superlative are not reliably backed by an allowlisted primary source here.

claims.claim_text: The module recommends using `aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent` on role trust policies for federated SSO users.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes / MFA row
claims.anchor_text: Even with SSO, require MFA via the `aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent` condition on trust policies for production account roles.
claims.suggested_rewrite: With IAM Identity Center or other federated identities, enforce MFA in the identity provider or pass MFA context as session attributes; `aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent` is not present for federated identities.
claims.rationale: AWS documents that `aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent` is not present for federated identities, so the original guidance is incorrect.

claims.claim_text: IAM Identity Center permission sets default to a 1-hour session duration and can be configured up to 12 hours.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes / session duration row
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly documents the default and maximum session duration for permission sets.

claims.claim_text: The module says `aws:SourceAccount` should always be added to cross-account trust policies and that any account constructing the right principal ARN could assume the role otherwise.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes / source account row
claims.anchor_text: Without source conditions, any account that can construct the right principal ARN could assume your role. Always add `aws:PrincipalOrgID` or `aws:SourceAccount`.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For org-internal trust, restrict access with `aws:PrincipalOrgID`; for third-party cross-account access, use `sts:ExternalId`; and for AWS service principals, use `aws:SourceArn` or `aws:SourceAccount` where the service documentation supports them.
claims.rationale: The original statement conflates third-party cross-account trust with AWS service-principal protections and overstates the role of `aws:SourceAccount`.

further_reading.title: Temporary Elevated Access for AWS Accounts
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the clearest primary-source overview of AWS just-in-time access concepts and workflow expectations.

further_reading.title: About Workload Identity Federation for GKE
further_reading.why_relevant: It explains the trust model, recommendations, and identity semantics behind GKE workload identity.

further_reading.title: Use Microsoft Entra Workload ID with AKS
further_reading.why_relevant: It documents the AKS OIDC federation model, labels, annotations, and token exchange flow.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Auditing
further_reading.why_relevant: It provides the vendor-neutral audit model needed to reason about identity tracing inside clusters.

notes: No shared citation pool was referenced in this bridge query, so `proposed_url` is populated directly. The opening anecdote, several 'Did You Know?' bullets, and two Common Mistakes rows contain either unsourced precision or partially incorrect guidance and should be rewritten before citation injection.
